questions.question: Which order best represents a browser rendering pipeline?
questions.explanation: Browsers first parse HTML into a DOM tree and CSS into a CSSOM, then resolve styles by matching selectors, compute layout (position and size), and finally paint pixels.

questions.question: What is the DOM?
options: A tree data structure representing the parsed HTML document
questions.explanation: The Document Object Model is a tree where each HTML element becomes a node. The DOM is the interface between HTML and the rest of the browser: CSS matches against it, JavaScript manipulates it, and layout computes geometry from it.

questions.question: In a toy block layout engine, what is the primary role of layout?
options: Compute x, y, width, height for each node
questions.explanation: Layout assigns geometry to styled nodes. Block layout stacks children vertically; inline layout flows horizontally. Each node gets an (x, y, w, h) box that the paint phase uses to draw pixels.

questions.question: What determines which CSS rule wins when multiple rules match the same element?
options: Specificity (inline, id, class, tag) and source order
The rule closest to the bottom of the file always wins
questions.explanation: CSS specificity is a triple: (inline, id, class, tag). Inline styles beat IDs, IDs beat classes, classes beat tags. When specificity is equal, the later rule in source order wins.

questions.question: Why is changing 'color' cheaper than changing 'width' in a browser?
options: Changing 'color' triggers only repaint; changing 'width' triggers layout (reflow) then repaint
questions.explanation: Layout-dependent properties (width, height, margin, padding) trigger a full reflow: the browser must recompute geometry for the affected subtree. Paint-only properties (color, background, opacity) only trigger repaint, which is much cheaper.
